stem:
A 26-year-old at 16 weeks gestation attends her GP for review. She smokes 15 cigarettes daily and has done so since age 17. She has tried unsuccessfully to quit during the first trimester using behavioural strategies alone. Her partner also smokes but is willing to support her quit attempt.

She has no other psychiatric or medical history, no significant past medical history, and is on no other medications apart from a pregnancy multivitamin. She asks specifically about pharmacological help because she feels behavioural advice has not been enough.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 82, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal HR 152 bpm
data_table.Examination: Mood euthymic, no anxiety symptoms, no psychomotor changes, weight tracking on the 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Booking bloods within normal limits, CO breath analyser 18 ppm (smoker range), urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological option?

options.text: Bupropion sustained-release 150 mg daily for one week then increased to twice daily
options.rationale: Bupropion is contraindicated in pregnancy because of seizure threshold effects and insufficient safety data; intermittent NRT is the AU pregnancy first-line. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline 2021)

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg titrated daily for 12 weeks as the most effective oral agent
options.rationale: Varenicline is contraindicated in pregnancy due to insufficient safety data and a signal for neuropsychiatric effects; AU guidance does not use it antenatally. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline 2021)

options.text: Intermittent nicotine replacement therapy by 2 mg lozenge as needed for cravings
options.rationale: RACGP and RANZCOG guidance prefers intermittent (not patch) NRT in pregnancy because lozenges and gum allow self-titration to minimum effective dose; varenicline and bupropion are contraindicated in pregnancy. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline 2021)

options.text: Electronic cigarettes with 12 mg/mL nicotine for use throughout the pregnancy day
options.rationale: AU guidance does not recommend electronic cigarettes in pregnancy because of inhalation product safety concerns and unregulated nicotine doses. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline 2021)

options.text: Continuous nicotine 21 mg patch for 16 hours daily across the next 8 to 12 weeks
options.rationale: Continuous patches give sustained nicotine that crosses the placenta; AU pregnancy guidance prefers intermittent dosing to minimise foetal nicotine exposure. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline 2021)

explanation.summary: Smoking in pregnancy increases miscarriage, IUGR, placental abruption, stillbirth and SIDS. Behavioural support is first-line; pharmacotherapy should be considered when behavioural alone fails. Intermittent NRT (lozenges, gum, inhaler) is preferred to patches because it allows lower total nicotine dose and self-titration. Varenicline and bupropion are contraindicated in pregnancy due to safety data gaps. Electronic cigarettes are not recommended. Partner cessation, nicotine replacement counselling, and ongoing CO breath monitoring increase quit success.
key_points: First-line: behavioural support and brief intervention.
Pharmacotherapy: intermittent NRT (lozenges, gum) preferred over patches.
Varenicline and bupropion contraindicated in pregnancy.
Electronic cigarettes not recommended.
explanation.pearls: AU pregnancy NRT: intermittent lozenges or gum over patches because self-titration reduces total foetal nicotine exposure.

stem:
A 24-year-old with a 2-year history of major depression presents to ED brought in by family. He has not spoken for 2 days, refuses food and water, and adopts unusual postures that he maintains for long periods. The family say he was started on no new medications recently and has not been on antipsychotics in the past.

On examination he stares without blinking, allows you to lift his arm into an unusual position which he maintains, and resists passive movement of his other limbs. His temperature is mildly elevated. There are no focal neurological signs. His urine drug screen is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 110, BP 140/86 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 99% in air, temperature 37.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Mute, posturing with waxy flexibility, negativism, staring without blinking, no focal neurology, hydration moderate to poor
data_table.Investigations: FBC and UEC within normal limits, CK 320 U/L (<170), urine drug screen negative, CT brain unremarkable, lumbar puncture pending if no improvement
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 100 mg mane, no antipsychotic exposure

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Mechanical ventilation and IV dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg for presumed malignant catatonia now
options.rationale: Dantrolene is for malignant hyperthermia; malignant catatonia treatment is benzodiazepines and ECT, not dantrolene, and pre-emptive intubation is not the first step in this stable patient. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Bilateral electroconvulsive therapy under general anaesthesia within the next 24 hours
options.rationale: ECT is the next escalation if benzodiazepines fail or the patient is malignant catatonia, but lorazepam challenge is the diagnostic and first-line therapeutic step. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg with promethazine 25 mg for rapid behavioural control
options.rationale: Antipsychotics in catatonia risk precipitating neuroleptic malignant syndrome and worsening rigidity; they are not first-line and should be avoided until catatonia is treated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g plus acyclovir 10 mg/kg for suspected encephalitis cover
options.rationale: Empirical infectious cover is given when meningoencephalitis is plausible, but unblinking stare with waxy flexibility and negativism in a known depression patient is more likely catatonia; CT and clinical picture do not suggest encephalitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Intravenous lorazepam 2 mg over 2 minutes with reassessment of catatonia at 15 minutes
options.rationale: The Bush-Francis lorazepam challenge is the AU first-line approach to catatonia: 1-2 mg IV with response (improved mutism, posturing, rigidity) at 10-15 minutes confirms the diagnosis and informs ongoing therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a clinical syndrome (not a discrete diagnosis) characterised by mutism, negativism, posturing, waxy flexibility, stupor or excitement. It is most commonly secondary to mood disorders (especially depression and bipolar), then schizophrenia, then medical causes (autoimmune encephalitis, NMS, drug withdrawal, electrolyte disturbances). The Bush-Francis lorazepam challenge (1-2 mg IV with reassessment in 10-15 minutes) is diagnostic and often therapeutic. Failure of response or malignant features (fever, autonomic instability, rigidity, raised CK) trigger ECT consideration. Avoid antipsychotics during active catatonia because they worsen rigidity and risk NMS.
key_points: Bush-Francis lorazepam challenge: 1-2 mg IV, reassess in 10-15 minutes.
Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and risk NMS - avoid during the syndrome.
Malignant features (fever, instability, rigidity, raised CK) escalate to ECT.
Causes: mood disorders > schizophrenia > medical (autoimmune, electrolytes).
explanation.pearls: Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV is the Bush-Francis challenge for catatonia; response at 10-15 minutes confirms diagnosis and starts treatment.

stem:
A 38-year-old with treatment-resistant schizophrenia has been on clozapine 400 mg nocte for 18 months with sustained remission of positive and negative symptoms. He attends his routine clozapine clinic. He has no fever, no infection, no mucositis, and feels his usual self. He takes no other regular medications.

His weekly FBC today shows a neutrophil count of 1.2 × 10^9/L (lower limit 2.0); his haemoglobin and platelets are within normal limits. Last week his neutrophil count was 2.3 × 10^9/L. A repeat FBC drawn 4 hours later in the same clinic confirms 1.2 × 10^9/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 98%, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: No oral lesions, no rash, no infection focus, mental state stable, no positive or negative symptoms
data_table.Investigations: Neutrophils 1.2 × 10^9/L on repeat (2.0-7.5), Hb 142 g/L, platelets 280 × 10^9/L, CRP <5 mg/L, no infection focus

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Continue clozapine but increase FBC monitoring to twice weekly with infection precautions
options.rationale: Australian Clopine and Clozaril protocols classify neutrophils 1.0-1.5 × 10^9/L as 'amber' alert: continue clozapine but switch to twice-weekly FBC until neutrophils recover above 2.0, with infection precautions. (Source: ClozapineConnect Australian Treatment Protocol 2024)

options.text: Stop clozapine immediately and refer to haematology for granulocyte colony stimulating factor
options.rationale: Cessation and G-CSF are reserved for 'red' alerts (neutrophils <0.5 × 10^9/L) or symptomatic patients; an amber-level fall in an asymptomatic patient is managed by enhanced monitoring. (Source: ClozapineConnect Australian Treatment Protocol 2024)

options.text: Reduce clozapine dose to 200 mg nocte and review FBC again in one week's time
options.rationale: Dose reduction does not improve haematological recovery because clozapine-induced neutropenia is idiosyncratic, not dose-related, and reduction risks symptomatic relapse. (Source: ClozapineConnect Australian Treatment Protocol 2024)

options.text: Switch to olanzapine 20 mg nocte from tonight without further clozapine dose given
options.rationale: Switching from clozapine in treatment-resistant schizophrenia after an amber alert is premature; the AU protocol allows continuation with enhanced monitoring and saves clozapine for the patient who has responded. (Source: ClozapineConnect Australian Treatment Protocol 2024)

options.text: Admit immediately for IV broad-spectrum antibiotics until neutrophils recover above 2.0
options.rationale: Empirical IV antibiotics are for neutropenic fever or significant infection; this patient is asymptomatic and afebrile, so admission is not indicated. (Source: ClozapineConnect Australian Treatment Protocol 2024)

explanation.summary: Clozapine-induced neutropenia and agranulocytosis is the highest-risk haematological adverse effect, with risk peaking in the first 18 weeks of treatment. Australian clozapine programs (ClozapineConnect, ClopineConnect) use a traffic-light system: green (neutrophils ≥2.0 × 10^9/L, continue and routine monitoring), amber (1.0-1.5, continue with twice-weekly FBC, infection precautions), red (<1.0 or signs of infection, stop clozapine, urgent haematology, possible G-CSF). Patients with confirmed agranulocytosis are usually permanently de-registered; rechallenge requires specialist haematology and psychiatry input.
key_points: Traffic light: green ≥2.0, amber 1.0-1.5, red <1.0 (×10^9/L).
Amber: continue clozapine, twice-weekly FBC, infection precautions.
Red or febrile: stop clozapine, urgent haematology, possible G-CSF.
Rechallenge after agranulocytosis requires specialist haematology and psychiatry.
explanation.pearls: Clozapine amber alert (neutrophils 1.0-1.5): continue dose with twice-weekly FBC and infection precautions; stop only at <1.0 or febrile.

stem:
A 62-year-old man presents to ED at 02:00 with 4 hours of central chest tightness radiating to the left jaw. He has hypertension and hyperlipidaemia and smokes 15 cigarettes daily. The pain partially settled with sublingual GTN but has now returned.

The baseline 12-lead ECG shows sinus rhythm with minor T-wave flattening laterally but no ST elevation. The initial high-sensitivity troponin T at 0 hours is 18 ng/L (URL 14 ng/L). A repeat at 1 hour is 32 ng/L. Renal function is normal and there is no recent significant illness or chronic kidney disease.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 138/82 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 98%, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic during pain, dual heart sounds with no murmur, chest clear, no calf swelling
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm with lateral T-wave flattening, 0-hour hs-cTnT 18 ng/L (<14), 1-hour hs-cTnT 32 ng/L (delta 14 ng/L), eGFR 88 mL/min, electrolytes within normal limits
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, sublingual GTN PRN

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Give intravenous thrombolysis with tenecteplase as he meets ACS criteria with raised troponin
options.rationale: Thrombolysis is for ST elevation MI when timely PCI is not available; NSTEMI is managed by antithrombotic therapy and invasive angiography, not thrombolysis. (Source: CSANZ 2025 Acute Coronary Syndrome Guideline)

options.text: Rule out NSTEMI on these troponin values and discharge home with GP follow-up
options.rationale: The 0/1-hour algorithm rules out only when 0-hour hs-cTnT is below the URL AND 1-hour delta is below threshold; this patient meets neither criterion. (Source: CSANZ 2025 Acute Coronary Syndrome Guideline)

options.text: Plan repeat troponin at 6 hours and admit only if the 6-hour value remains elevated
options.rationale: The conventional 0/3 or 0/6 hour algorithm is superseded by the 0/1 hour high-sensitivity protocol in AU EDs when hs-cTn is available, which already provides the diagnosis. (Source: CSANZ 2025 Acute Coronary Syndrome Guideline)

options.text: Arrange outpatient computed tomography coronary angiography within the next week
options.rationale: CT coronary angiography is for low-risk chest pain after troponin rule-out; once NSTEMI is ruled in, invasive coronary angiography is the procedure of choice. (Source: CSANZ 2025 Acute Coronary Syndrome Guideline)

options.text: Diagnose NSTEMI, give DAPT plus parenteral anticoagulant, and refer for early invasive angiography
options.rationale: ESC/CSANZ 0/1-hour algorithm rules in NSTEMI when the 0-hour hs-cTnT is above the URL with a 1-hour delta ≥5 ng/L; early invasive angiography within 24 hours plus DAPT and parenteral anticoagulation is the AU approach. (Source: CSANZ 2025 Acute Coronary Syndrome Guideline)

explanation.summary: The 0/1-hour high-sensitivity troponin algorithm streamlines acute chest pain triage in Australian EDs. Rule-out requires 0-hour hs-cTnT <5 ng/L if symptom onset >3 hours, or 0-hour <12 ng/L with 1-hour delta <3 ng/L. Rule-in requires 0-hour ≥52 ng/L OR 1-hour delta ≥5 ng/L (men) or ≥3 ng/L (women) above an above-URL baseline. The 'observation zone' between rule-out and rule-in triggers further serial troponin, imaging, or risk stratification. NSTEMI rule-in mandates DAPT, parenteral anticoagulation (heparin or enoxaparin or fondaparinux), and angiography timing by risk class.
key_points: 0/1-hour rule-in: 0-hour above URL plus 1-hour delta ≥5 ng/L.
0/1-hour rule-out: very low 0-hour OR very low + delta <3 ng/L.
NSTEMI: DAPT, parenteral anticoagulant, early invasive within 24 hours for high-risk.
Thrombolysis is for STEMI without timely PCI access, not NSTEMI.
explanation.pearls: hs-cTnT 0/1-hour: delta ≥5 ng/L above an above-URL baseline rules in NSTEMI and triggers early invasive angiography.

stem:
A 35-year-old presents to an outer-suburban GP clinic 15 minutes after eating cashew nuts at a restaurant. He has known peanut allergy and now reports tightness in his throat and difficulty breathing. He has urticarial wheals over his trunk and arms. He is alert but anxious.

He has no diagnosed cardiac disease. He has no regular medications. His weight at his last GP visit was 80 kg. The practice has a single 1 mg/mL adrenaline ampoule and a 1 mL syringe; an EpiPen and EpiPen Jr are also stocked.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124, BP 86/52 mmHg, RR 28 with audible wheeze, SpO2 92% in air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Generalised urticaria, perioral swelling, expiratory wheeze bilaterally, no stridor, alert, capillary refill 3 seconds
data_table.Investigations: Bedside only, ECG sinus tachycardia, no other immediate results

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first dose of adrenaline?

options.text: Adrenaline 1:1000 0.3 mg IM (300 mcg) into the mid-anterolateral thigh, repeatable
options.rationale: 0.3 mg is the paediatric EpiPen dose for 20 kg or above; the adult standard ASCIA dose is 0.5 mg of 1:1000 IM. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023

options.text: Adrenaline 1:1000 0.5 mg IM (500 mcg) into the mid-anterolateral thigh, repeatable
options.rationale: ASCIA adult anaphylaxis dose is 0.5 mg (500 mcg) IM 1:1000 into the mid-anterolateral thigh, repeated every 5 minutes if symptoms persist, before considering IV adrenaline infusion. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023

options.text: Adrenaline 1:10 000 1 mg IV (1000 mcg) bolus over 1 minute by intravenous access
options.rationale: Intravenous bolus adrenaline 1 mg is for cardiac arrest, not anaphylaxis; IV adrenaline in anaphylaxis is given by titrated infusion in a monitored setting, after IM has failed. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023

options.text: Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV plus chlorphenamine 10 mg IV before any adrenaline dose
options.rationale: Steroids and antihistamines do not treat acute anaphylaxis cardiorespiratory compromise; adrenaline must be given first and is never delayed by adjuncts. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg with face mask oxygen and admit to the local hospital ward
options.rationale: Nebulised salbutamol is an adjunct for persistent bronchospasm AFTER adrenaline; it does not address hypotension or airway oedema and does not replace adrenaline. (Source: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023)
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023

explanation.summary: Adult anaphylaxis is treated by ASCIA: IM adrenaline 0.5 mg of 1:1000 (500 mcg) into the mid-anterolateral thigh, repeated every 5 minutes if symptoms persist. Lay the patient flat with legs raised, give high-flow oxygen, and obtain IV access. IV adrenaline infusion (titrated to response, typically starting at 1 mcg/min) is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts, not primary treatment, and do not reverse cardiorespiratory compromise. Biphasic reactions occur in approximately 5-20 percent within 4-6 hours; observe for 4 hours minimum.
key_points: Adult ASCIA dose: 0.5 mg (500 mcg) IM 1:1000 into mid-anterolateral thigh.
Repeat every 5 minutes if persistent; lie flat with legs raised.
IV adrenaline infusion only after IM failure in a monitored setting.
Observe minimum 4 hours for biphasic reaction (5-20%).
explanation.pearls: Adult anaphylaxis: 0.5 mg IM adrenaline 1:1000 every 5 minutes if persistent; IV bolus is for arrest, not anaphylaxis.

sources.label: ASCIA Guidelines for Acute Management of Anaphylaxis 2023

stem:
A 24-year-old presents to ED 6 hours after taking what she states was 30 paracetamol 500 mg tablets in a single ingestion. She is now nauseated but not vomiting. There is no co-ingestion identified on history or urine drug screen, and she takes no regular medications. She weighs 60 kg.

A paracetamol level taken on arrival at 6 hours post-ingestion is 200 mg/L. INR is 1.1. ALT and creatinine are within normal limits. The treating nomogram in your ED is the Australian Rumack-Matthew '150 line' (paracetamol 150 mg/L at 4 hours).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mildly nauseated, no abdominal tenderness, no jaundice, no encephalopathy, alert and orientated
data_table.Investigations: Paracetamol 200 mg/L at 6 hours, INR 1.1 (0.8-1.2), ALT 28 U/L (5-30), creatinine 70 micromol/L (60-110), urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Withhold N-acetylcysteine because the level is below the standard 200 mg/L 4-hour line
options.rationale: The 200 mg/L line is the 4-hour value of the higher-risk North American nomogram; AU uses the 150 mg/L 4-hour line and at 6 hours the threshold is around 125 mg/L. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Commence intravenous N-acetylcysteine using the 21-hour two-bag Australian protocol
options.rationale: On the Australian Rumack-Matthew 150 mg/L line, the 6-hour threshold is approximately 125 mg/L; this patient's level of 200 mg/L is above the line and requires NAC. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer single-dose activated charcoal 50 g orally before any NAC is started
options.rationale: Activated charcoal is considered within 1-2 hours of ingestion; at 6 hours post-ingestion it adds no benefit and delays NAC which is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Repeat the paracetamol level at 12 hours and start NAC only if the level is still high
options.rationale: NAC efficacy is greatest within 8-10 hours of ingestion; delaying for a further level wastes the protective window when the current level is already above the AU threshold. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Refer immediately for haemodialysis to remove circulating paracetamol from the blood
options.rationale: Haemodialysis for paracetamol is reserved for massive ingestion (>500 mg/kg) with very high levels (>1000 mg/L) or established acidosis with coma; this patient has a single moderate ingestion and is alert. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Paracetamol overdose management in Australia uses the 150 mg/L Rumack-Matthew nomogram from 4 to 16 hours post-ingestion; any level above the line triggers N-acetylcysteine. The 21-hour two-bag protocol gives 200 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours. Earlier NAC (within 8 hours) prevents the majority of hepatotoxicity; later treatment still benefits established disease. Massive ingestion (>30 g or >500 mg/kg) may justify a 3-bag higher-dose protocol. Repeat levels and LFTs at end of NAC determine completion or extension.
key_points: AU nomogram: 150 mg/L 4-hour line; treat any level above.
21-hour 2-bag protocol: 200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours.
NAC most effective within 8 hours of ingestion.
Massive ingestion (>500 mg/kg) may use 3-bag higher-dose regimen.
explanation.pearls: AU paracetamol nomogram 150 mg/L line at 4 hours = NAC; at 6 hours threshold is approximately 125 mg/L.

sources.label: NSW Poisons Information Centre Paracetamol Guideline

stem:
A 34-year-old healthcare worker presents for review after a routine occupational health screen. She was born in Australia, but worked in a high-prevalence overseas region for 18 months and returned 6 months ago. Her QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus is positive and her chest X-ray is normal. She has no cough, no fever, no weight loss, and no night sweats.

She is otherwise well, with no liver disease, no alcohol overuse, no pregnancy or contraception requirements, and no other medications. Her HIV test is negative. Her baseline LFTs and renal function are within normal limits.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: No lymphadenopathy, chest clear, abdomen soft, no organomegaly, no skin lesions
data_table.Investigations: QFT-Plus positive, chest X-ray normal, FBC and UEC within normal limits, ALT 18 U/L (5-30), bilirubin within normal limits, HIV negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate latent TB treatment regimen?

options.text: Isoniazid plus ethambutol daily for 12 months with monthly visual acuity monitoring
options.rationale: Ethambutol is not used for LTBI; it is part of active TB therapy and adds optic neuropathy risk without benefit in latent infection. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017

options.text: Isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol daily for 6 months full RIPE course
options.rationale: RIPE quadruple therapy is for ACTIVE TB; LTBI treatment is single or dual agent because the burden of disease is much lower and toxicity must be minimised. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017

options.text: Rifampicin 600 mg daily monotherapy for 6 months as the single-agent shorter regimen
options.rationale: The AU rifampicin LTBI regimen is 4 months not 6 months; 6 months prolongs hepatotoxicity exposure without added efficacy. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017

options.text: Isoniazid 300 mg daily plus pyridoxine 25 mg daily for 6 months total duration
options.rationale: Australian guidance lists 6 months of daily isoniazid with pyridoxine as a first-line LTBI regimen; alternative is 3 months of weekly isoniazid plus rifapentine, or 4 months of daily rifampicin. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017

options.text: No drug treatment; surveillance with annual chest X-ray and symptom review for 2 years
options.rationale: Active surveillance alone is not adequate when QFT-Plus is positive and risk factors (healthcare work, recent return from high-prevalence area) are present; treatment reduces progression to active disease by 60-90%. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017)
source_refs: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017

explanation.summary: Latent tuberculosis is diagnosed by a positive interferon-gamma release assay (QFT-Plus) or tuberculin skin test in an asymptomatic patient with a normal chest X-ray and no clinical features of active TB. Australian first-line regimens are isoniazid 6 months (with pyridoxine), rifampicin 4 months, or isoniazid plus rifapentine weekly for 12 weeks. Choice is informed by drug interactions, hepatotoxicity risk, pregnancy, and patient preference. Baseline LFTs and renal function are checked and monitored. Treatment reduces progression to active disease by 60-90 percent and is offered to all eligible adults with LTBI plus risk factors.
key_points: AU first-line LTBI regimens: isoniazid 6 m, rifampicin 4 m, or isoniazid + rifapentine 12 weekly doses.
Pyridoxine 25 mg daily with isoniazid prevents peripheral neuropathy.
Baseline and 4-weekly LFT monitoring during isoniazid.
Treatment cuts active TB progression by 60-90%.
explanation.pearls: AU LTBI: 6 m isoniazid + pyridoxine, OR 4 m rifampicin, OR 12-week weekly isoniazid + rifapentine.

sources.label: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee LTBI Guideline 2017

stem:
A 42-year-old with Addison's disease on hydrocortisone 20 mg morning and 10 mg evening, plus fludrocortisone 100 mcg daily, presents 18 hours into a gastroenteritis illness. She has had 8 episodes of vomiting and has not been able to keep down her morning hydrocortisone dose. She did not have her steroid emergency injection at home because the family could not find it.

On arrival to the emergency department she is drowsy but rousable. She is afebrile. Her abdomen is soft. There is no chest or respiratory infection identified clinically.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124, BP 82/48 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 seconds, no peritonism, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 3.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.4), sodium 128 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 5.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), urea 12 mmol/L (3.0-8.0), creatinine 110 micromol/L (60-110), VBG pH 7.30 HCO3 18 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat followed by 0.9% saline bolus 1 L over 30 minutes
options.rationale: AU adrenal crisis pathway is IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat (then 200 mg over 24 hours) plus prompt isotonic saline resuscitation to correct hypovolaemia, hyponatraemia and hypoglycaemia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Oral hydrocortisone 40 mg now and reassess in 2 hours after rehydration with oral fluids
options.rationale: Oral steroids and oral rehydration are inappropriate in adrenal crisis with vomiting and hypotension; absorption is unreliable and the patient needs immediate IV access. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Intravenous fludrocortisone 100 mcg with isotonic saline 500 mL over the first hour
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone is not available IV and is unnecessary acutely because high-dose hydrocortisone has sufficient mineralocorticoid activity at 100 mg doses. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Intravenous insulin and dextrose to correct hyperkalaemia before any steroid is given
options.rationale: The hyperkalaemia in adrenal crisis is secondary to cortisol and aldosterone deficiency; it resolves with hydrocortisone and saline, and standard insulin-dextrose risks rebound hypoglycaemia in this already hypoglycaemic patient. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Intravenous calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% over 10 minutes before fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Calcium gluconate is used for cardioprotection in life-threatening hyperkalaemia with ECG changes; this patient does not yet have ECG features and steroid plus saline are higher priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is precipitated by intercurrent illness, surgery, missed steroid doses, vomiting, or trauma in patients with primary or secondary adrenal insufficiency. Classic features are hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, and metabolic acidosis. Treatment is immediate IV hydrocortisone (100 mg stat then 200 mg per 24 h as infusion or 50 mg six-hourly), isotonic saline resuscitation, and treatment of any precipitant. After acute recovery, double or triple the regular steroid dose during intercurrent illness ('sick day rules'); ensure the patient has an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit and a Medic-Alert.
key_points: Adrenal crisis: hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia.
IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat then 200 mg over 24 hours.
Saline resuscitation and dextrose for hypoglycaemia.
Sick day rules: double or triple steroid dose for febrile illness.
explanation.pearls: Adrenal crisis: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat + 0.9% saline bolus before any other intervention; hyperkalaemia self-corrects with steroid replacement.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia adrenal insufficiency statement

stem:
A 42-year-old man with type 1 diabetes presents with vomiting, abdominal pain, polyuria, and Kussmaul breathing after running out of insulin 36 hours ago. He weighs 80 kg. He has had no recent diuretic use and no chronic gastrointestinal losses other than the current vomiting.

Venous blood gas shows pH 7.12, bicarbonate 8 mmol/L. Capillary glucose is 24 mmol/L, ketones 5.4 mmol/L. Sodium is 138 mmol/L. Critically, the initial potassium is 2.9 mmol/L and the ECG shows ST depression with a U wave. He is alert and orientated (GCS 15) and has IV access in place. The standard ADS DKA pathway is available.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 128, BP 96/60 mmHg, RR 32 Kussmaul, SpO2 99% in air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 seconds, alert, mild diffuse abdominal tenderness without peritonism
data_table.Investigations: pH 7.12, bicarbonate 8 mmol/L (22-32), glucose 24 mmol/L, ketones 5.4 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 2.9 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), corrected anion gap 24, ECG ST depression with U waves
data_table.Medications: Insulin glargine (ran out), insulin aspart (ran out)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: IV saline 1 L plus 40 mmol potassium chloride over 1 hour; hold insulin until K ≥3.5 mmol/L
options.rationale: ADS DKA protocol holds insulin until potassium is at or above 3.5 mmol/L because insulin drives potassium intracellularly and risks fatal hypokalaemia; potassium-replete saline runs first. (Source: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020)
source_refs: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020

options.text: IV insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/h commencing immediately at recognition of DKA
options.rationale: Insulin in the setting of potassium <3.3 mmol/L precipitates further intracellular potassium shift and can trigger ventricular arrhythmia; the ADS pathway holds insulin until potassium ≥3.5 mmol/L. (Source: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020)
source_refs: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol bolus to correct severe acidosis before insulin or fluids
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is only considered for adult DKA with pH <6.9 and persistent shock; routine use worsens intracellular acidosis and aggravates hypokalaemia, which is already critical here. (Source: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020)
source_refs: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020

options.text: IV 0.9% saline 2 L bolus then immediate insulin infusion before any potassium replacement
options.rationale: Aggressive saline without potassium plus immediate insulin will drive potassium even lower from an already 2.9 mmol/L baseline, with ECG changes already present; potassium replacement must come first. (Source: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020)
source_refs: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020

options.text: Subcutaneous insulin aspart 10 units stat plus oral potassium replacement in 200 mL water
options.rationale: Subcutaneous insulin and oral potassium are inappropriate in active DKA with hypokalaemia, vomiting and shock; absorption is unreliable and IV therapy is required. (Source: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020)
source_refs: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020

explanation.summary: Adult DKA management in Australia (ADS protocol) follows fluid, insulin, potassium, glucose, and search for precipitant. Critically, when initial potassium is <3.3 mmol/L, insulin must be HELD until potassium is replaced to at least 3.5 mmol/L, because insulin drives further intracellular shift and causes potentially fatal hypokalaemia. Replace potassium at 20-40 mmol per litre of crystalloid; rates above 10 mmol/h need cardiac monitoring. Once potassium ≥3.5, start fixed-rate insulin 0.1 unit/kg/h. Add 5% dextrose to fluids once glucose ≤14 mmol/L. Bicarbonate reserved for pH <6.9 with shock.
key_points: ADS DKA: hold insulin if K <3.3 mmol/L until replaced to ≥3.5.
Replace K at 20-40 mmol/L in crystalloid; cardiac monitor if >10 mmol/h.
Fixed-rate insulin 0.1 unit/kg/h once potassium safe.
Add 5% dextrose to fluid once glucose ≤14 mmol/L.
explanation.pearls: DKA with potassium <3.3 mmol/L: replace potassium first, hold insulin until K ≥3.5 mmol/L (ADS protocol).

sources.label: ADS Australian Diabetes Society DKA Guideline 2020

stem:
A 48-year-old presents to your endocrinology outpatient clinic for review. She was diagnosed with a unilateral 4 cm right adrenal mass on a CT abdomen done after recurrent panic-like episodes. Plasma metanephrines are markedly elevated, consistent with phaeochromocytoma. Her GP started metoprolol 50 mg twice daily one week ago to control her tachycardia.

Her home BP readings have actually worsened, ranging 165-180 systolic and 100-110 diastolic. She has no history of asthma. She is awaiting surgical planning. She has no other medications and no significant past medical history.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 172/108 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: No bruits, no thyromegaly, no acanthosis, no orthostatic drop measured today, abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Plasma free normetanephrine 4500 pmol/L (<900), CT abdomen 4 cm right adrenal mass, UEC and FBC within normal limits, ECG sinus rhythm
data_table.Medications: Metoprolol 50 mg twice daily (started 1 week ago)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?

options.text: Plan immediate surgical adrenalectomy this week without further antihypertensive change
options.rationale: Surgery without adequate alpha blockade for 10-14 days carries a high risk of intraoperative hypertensive crisis, arrhythmia and death; pre-operative blockade is mandatory. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement

options.text: Continue metoprolol and add phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily, escalating as tolerated
options.rationale: Continuing beta blockade alongside the start of alpha blockade is not safer than the AU recommended sequence of stopping beta first, then starting alpha, then re-introducing beta once alpha is established. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement

options.text: Increase metoprolol to 100 mg twice daily and add amlodipine 5 mg daily for BP control
options.rationale: Escalating beta blockade in phaeochromocytoma without alpha blockade worsens the unopposed alpha state and accelerated hypertension risk; calcium channel blockers are adjuncts, not the primary fix here. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement

options.text: Stop metoprolol now and start phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily, titrated to symptoms
options.rationale: Beta blockade without prior alpha blockade in phaeochromocytoma causes unopposed alpha vasoconstriction and worsened hypertension; the AU approach is to STOP metoprolol, start phenoxybenzamine, and only add a beta blocker once alpha blockade is established. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement

options.text: Substitute hydralazine 25 mg three times daily for metoprolol and continue current plan
options.rationale: Hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia and is not a controlled choice in phaeochromocytoma; phenoxybenzamine is the AU preferred preoperative alpha blocker. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma requires preoperative alpha-adrenergic blockade for 10-14 days before any beta blockade is added, to prevent unopposed alpha vasoconstriction and hypertensive crisis. The AU first-line agent is phenoxybenzamine (irreversible non-selective alpha blocker); doxazosin is an alternative. Beta blockade is added only AFTER adequate alpha blockade, to control reflex tachycardia. High sodium and fluid intake during the alpha blockade phase prevents volume contraction-related hypotension. Surgery (typically laparoscopic adrenalectomy) is planned when BP and heart rate targets are met.
key_points: Alpha block first, beta block only after alpha block is established.
Phenoxybenzamine first-line; doxazosin alternative.
10-14 days of alpha block before surgery.
Liberal salt and fluid intake during alpha block to prevent hypotension.
explanation.pearls: Phaeochromocytoma preop: stop any premature beta blocker; start phenoxybenzamine for 10-14 days, then re-add beta blocker only once alpha block is established.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia phaeochromocytoma statement
